mapping.a_element: Tanner/factor graph associated with the LDGM code
iterative message passing (belief propagation) over the graph
mapping.b_element: best-response dynamics iterated over the network

mapping.a_element: discrete Density Evolution predicting decoder behavior on large random graphs
mapping.b_element: deterministic approximation predicting equilibrium frequencies on large sparse random networks

brief_justification: The only overlap is generic — both run iterative local updates on a graph and use a mean-field/large-graph asymptotic argument — but the governing machinery differs fundamentally: paper A constructs CSS quantum error-correcting codes and decodes them by probabilistic belief-propagation optimized via density evolution over a depolarizing channel, whereas paper B analyzes existence and convergence of Nash equilibria under best-response dynamics in heterogeneous network games. Code construction plus channel-optimized inference does not play the same role as game-theoretic equilibrium analysis, so no coherent role-for-role mapping of the actual mechanism holds.
